items.title: Smooth trend forecasting
items.description: Economists and product teams fit low-degree polynomials over time to forecast short-term trends when the underlying process is smooth but not strictly linear.

items.title: Hardware and sensor calibration
items.description: Embedded systems store a handful of polynomial coefficients mapping raw sensor readings to physical units—fast to evaluate and easy to ship.

items.title: Baseline non-linear regression
items.description: Before trying splines, kernels, or neural nets, a degree-2 or degree-3 polynomial often reveals whether simple curvature explains the signal.

items.title: Response-surface modeling
items.description: In experimental design, quadratic polynomials in two or three factors approximate how an output (yield, latency) responds to controlled inputs.
